name: Controlled Use of Fire
key_figures: Homo erectus (earliest evidence); later Homo sapiens
summary: Fire evidence from Wonderwerk Cave, South Africa dates to ~1 million years ago. Deliberate control enabled cooking, warmth, and protection — reshaping human biology and culture.
key_facts: Key sites: Wonderwerk Cave, South Africa

name: Agriculture and Domestication
key_figures: Multiple independent centres: Fertile Crescent, China, Mesoamerica, Africa
summary: Farming emerged independently in at least seven regions. Crop cultivation and animal domestication enabled permanent settlements, population growth, and the first cities.
key_facts: First crops: wheat, barley, millet, rice
First animals: goats, sheep, cattle, pigs

name: The Wheel
period: c. 3,500 BCE (wheeled vehicle); c. 5,500 BCE (potter's wheel)
key_figures: Mesopotamia and the Eurasian steppe (multiple independent inventions debated)
summary: The wheel appeared in Mesopotamia ~3500 BCE. The key insight was the axle, allowing free rotation. The wheel's principle underpins gears, turbines, engines, and all machinery.
key_facts: Americas and Africa developed without it

name: Writing Systems
period: c. 3,400 – 3,000 BCE (Sumerian cuneiform); c. 3,200 BCE (Egyptian hieroglyphs)
key_figures: Sumer (cuneiform); Egypt (hieroglyphs); China and Mesoamerica (independent inventions)
summary: Writing was invented at least four times independently. A Cuneiform began as an accounting system. The alphabet (~1800 BCE) reduced thousands of signs to a small phonetic set.
key_facts: Alphabet invented in ancient Levant ~1800 BCE

name: Mathematics and the Concept of Zero
period: c. 3,000 BCE (counting systems); 628 CE (Brahmagupta formalises zero)
key_figures: Babylonian, Egyptian, Indian (Brahmagupta), and Maya mathematicians
summary: hmagupta formalised zero as a number in 628 CE. The Hindu- bicnumeral system reached Europevia Islamicscholars, lacing Roman numeralsand enabling modern calculation.
key_facts: Hindu-Arabic numerals spread via Islamic world

name: Paper
period: c. 105 CE (official Chinese invention attributed to Cai Lun); spread to Europe by 12th century
key_figures: Cai Lun (Han Dynasty China); later Islamic and European paper mills
summary: Cai Lun made paper from plant fibres in 105 CE. Paper reached the Islamic world by the 8th century, and Europe by the 12th — making knowledge cheaper and more portable.
key_facts: Invented in Han Dynasty China, 105 CE
Reached Europe via Islamic Spain: 12 th century
Made books affordable before printing press

name: The Compass
period: c. 200 BCE (early Chinese lodestone compass); c. 1100 CE (navigational use in Europe and China)
key_figures: Han Dynasty China; independently developed in Europe c. 12th century
summary: First a Chinese divination tool, the magnetic compass was E adapted for maritime navigation by ~1040 CE. It enabled open- ocean sailing and the Age of Exploration. 上

name: Gunpowder
period: c. 850 CE (Chinese alchemical discovery); widespread military use from c. 1000 CE
key_figures: Chinese alchemists (Tang Dynasty); spread to Islamic world and Europe by 13th century
summary: covered seeking immortality, gunpowder's formula (sulphur, rcoal, saltpetre) appeared in Chinese texts by 850 CE. nonsmademedieval fortificationsobsolete.
key_facts: Discovered by Tang Dynasty alchemists ~850 CE
Military use in China: 1 oth century
Reached Europe via Islamic scholars: 13 th century
Later enabled mining and civil engineering

name: The Printing Press
period: c. 1440 CE (Gutenberg's movable-type press in Europe); movable type in China c. 1040 CE
key_figures: Johannes Gutenberg (Europe); Bi Sheng (China, c. 1040 CE, earlier movable type)
summary: enberg's movable-type press (~1440) reduced book costs by 6 within decades, enabling mass literacy. The Gutenberg Bible 455) was among the first major printed books.
key_facts: Movable type in China: Bi Sheng, ~1040 CE

name: The Scientific Method
period: c. 1000–1600 CE (development from Ibn al-Haytham to Bacon, Galileo, Descartes)
key_figures: Ibn al-Haytham (optics, c. 1021); Francis Bacon; Galileo Galilei; René Descartes
summary: al-Haytham pioneered systematicexperimentation in optics 021 CE). Bacon, Galileo, and Descartes developed the othetico-deductive model that drivesmodern science.
key_facts: Ibn al-Haytham's'Book of Optics': c. 1021 CE

name: The Telescope
period: 1608 (first patent, Netherlands); 1609 (Galileo's astronomical use)
key_figures: Hans Lipperhey (inventor, Netherlands); Galileo Galilei (first systematic astronomical use)
summary: Galileo turned the telescope skyward in 1609, observing moons of Jupiter, lunar mountains, and phases of Venus — all challenging the Earth-centred model of the cosmos.
key_facts: Revealed moons of Jupiter, craters on Moon

name: The Microscope
period: c. 1590 (earliest compound microscope); 1670s (Antoni van Leeuwenhoek's improvements)
key_figures: Zacharias Janssen (compound microscope, c. 1590); Antoni van Leeuwenhoek (high-power single-lens)
summary: uwenhoek built microscopes magnifying up to 270 x in the Os, first observing bacteria — 'animalcules'. This discovery s the first step toward germ theory.
key_facts: Leeuwenhoek magnified up to 270 x, 1670 s
First observations of bacteria and protozoa
Electron microscopes now image individual atoms

name: Newtonian Mechanics
period: 1687 (Principia Mathematica published)
key_figures: Isaac Newton
summary: vton's Principia (1687) unified terrestrial and celestial sics in one framework. He also invented calculus as a tool describing continuously changing quantities.

name: Steam Engine
period: 1712 (Newcomen atmospheric engine); 1769 (Watt's separate condenser patent)
key_figures: Thomas Newcomen; James Watt (improved design); Richard Trevithick (high-pressure steam)
summary: t'sseparatecondenser(1769) dramaticallyincreased ciency, making steam practical for factories and railways. owered the Industrial Revolution.

name: Electricity Generation and Distribution
period: 1831 (Faraday's electromagnetic induction); 1880s (Edison and Tesla's competing systems)
key_figures: Michael Faraday; Thomas Edison; Nikola Tesla; George Westinghouse
summary: aday's 1831 discovery of electromagnetic induction underlies ry generator. The AC/Dc 'War of the Currents' in the 1880 s led with AC winning for long-distance distribution.
key_facts: Edison's DC system vs Tesla's AC: 1880 s-1890 s

name: Electromagnetism
period: 1820–1865 (Ørsted, Ampère, Faraday, Maxwell)
key_figures: Hans Christian Ørsted; André-Marie Ampère; Michael Faraday; James Clerk Maxwell
summary: xwell's four equations (1865) unified electricity, magnetism, Ilight asaspects of one phenomenon, predicting ctromagneticwaves andlaying thebasis formodern tronics.
key_facts: Predicted light, radio, X-rays as EM waves
Foundation of all motors, radios, Wi-F

name: Internal Combustion Engine
period: 1859–1876 (first practical designs: Lenoir, Otto, Beau de Rochas)
key_figures: Étienne Lenoir; Nikolaus Otto (four-stroke cycle, 1876); Rudolf Diesel (1897)
summary: olaus Otto's four-stroke cycle (1876) became the basis for st gasoline engines. Karl Benz applied it to a horseless riage in 1885, creating the first automobile.
key_facts: Compact design enabled cars and aircraft

name: Automobile
period: 1885–1886 (Benz Patent-Motorwagen); mass production from 1908 (Ford Model T)
key_figures: Karl Benz; Gottlieb Daimler; Henry Ford (mass production)
summary: z's Patent-Motorwagen (1885) was the first true automobile. d's Model T (1908) and moving assembly line (1913) made cars ordable for ordinary working people.
key_facts: ~1.35 M road fatalities globally per year

name: Airplane
period: 1903 (Wright Brothers' first powered flight, Kitty Hawk, North Carolina)
key_figures: Orville and Wilbur Wright; later: Louis Blériot, Anthony Fokker, Frank Whittle (jet engine)
summary: Wright Brothers'12-second flighton 17 December 1903 was hittle, 1937) made modern aviation possible.
key_facts: First powered, heavier-than-air flight. The jet engine

name: Refrigeration
period: 1834 (Perkins' vapour-compression cycle patent); widespread use from 1920s–1950s
key_figures: Jacob Perkins; Carl von Linde; Clarence Birdseye (commercial frozen food)
summary: thanical refrigeration (Perkins, 1834) and Birdseye's quick zing(1920 s) transformed foodsafety andenabled global d chains for food and vaccines.
key_facts: Enables vaccine and medicine cold chains

name: Vaccination
period: 1796 (Jenner's smallpox vaccine); mass immunisation programmes from the 19th century
key_figures: Edward Jenner (smallpox); Louis Pasteur (rabies, cholera); Jonas Salk (polio)
summary: her's 1796 cowpox inoculation was theworld's firstvaccine. allpoxwas declared eradicated by WHO in 1980-the first man disease fully eliminated.
key_facts: M RNA vaccinesdeveloped in 2020 for COVi D-19

name: Antibiotics
period: 1928 (Fleming's penicillin discovery); 1940s (mass production and clinical use)
key_figures: Alexander Fleming (discovery, 1928); Howard Florey and Ernst Chain (development, 1940s)
summary: Fleming observed penicillin inhibiting bacteria in 1928. Florey and Chain purified it by 1940. Mass production during WWlI saved countless lives from infected wounds.

name: Germ Theory of Disease
period: 1850s–1880s (Pasteur and Koch's foundational work)
key_figures: Louis Pasteur; Robert Koch; Ignaz Semmelweis; John Snow
summary: teur's experiments(1859-1861) established that specific roorganisms cause specific diseases. Koch identified the TB illus (1882) and cholera bacterium (1883).
key_facts: Snow traced cholera to water pump: 1854
Koch identified TB and cholera bactenia

name: Anaesthesia
period: 1842–1847 (Crawford Long, William Morton, James Young Simpson)
key_figures: Crawford Long (ether, 1842); William Morton (first public demonstration, 1846); James Young Simpson (chloroform)
summary: Morton's public demonstration of ether anaesthesia at Massachusetts General Hospital on 16 October 1846 is the conventional start date. Chloroform anaesthesia, introduced by Simpson in 1847, transformed surgery worldwide.
key_facts: First ether use in surgery: Long, 1842
Modern anaesthesia uses sophisticated drug combos

name: X-Rays and Medical Imaging
period: 1895 (Röntgen's discovery of X-rays); CT (1971); MRI (1977); ultrasound (1950s)
key_figures: Wilhelm Conrad Röntgen (X-rays, 1895); Godfrey Hounsfield and Allan Cormack (CT, 1971)
summary: tgen produced the first X-ray image — of his wife's hand hin weeks of discovering X-rays in November 1895. CT nning (197 l) added cross-sectional imaging.
key_facts: Now includes PET, ultrasound, functional MRI

name: Sanitation and Clean Water Systems
period: 1850s–1900 (Great Stink, London sewer system); 20th century global expansion
key_figures: Joseph Bazalgette (London sewers); Edwin Chadwick; John Snow; Lemuel Shattuck
summary: don's Great Stink (1858) drove construction of Bazalgette's /er network. Water chlorination in Jersey City (1908) duced immediate dramatic fallsin waterborme disease.
key_facts: 2 B people still lack safe water (WHO 2023)

name: DNA Structure and Molecular Genetics
period: 1953 (Watson and Crick's double helix structure); preceded by Avery (1944) and Franklin's X-ray work
key_figures: James Watson, Francis Crick; Rosalind Franklin (X-ray crystallography); Erwin Chargaff; Oswald Avery
summary: Watson and Crick deduced the double helix structure in 1953 partly using Rosalind Franklin's crucial X-ray diffraction data. This explained heredity at the molecular level.
key_facts: Avery showed DNA carries genetic info: 1944
Watson and Crick double helix: April 1953
Opened molecular biology. biotechnology, CRi SPR

name: Human Genome Sequencing
period: 1990–2003 (Human Genome Project); first complete sequence 2022
key_figures: Human Genome Project consortium (NIH, Wellcome Trust, multiple nations); Craig Venter (Celera Genomics)
summary: Human Genome Project sequenced 3 billion base pairs over 13 HGP launched: 1990 rs. Genome sequencing cost fell from $3 B in 2003 to under 10 by 2024, transforming personalised medicine.

name: CRISPR Gene Editing
period: 2012 (CRISPR-Cas9 adapted for gene editing); first clinical applications from 2020s
key_figures: Jennifer Doudna and Emmanuelle Charpentier (Nobel Prize 2020); Feng Zhang (independent parallel work)
summary: idna and Charpentier's 2012 Science paper showed CRISPR-Cas Boudna & Charpentier CRISPR paper: 2012 Id be reprogrammed to edit any DNA sequence. First CRISPR rapy (sickle cell) FDA-approved in 2023.

name: The Periodic Table
period: 1869 (Mendeleev's publication); completed across the 20th century
key_figures: Dmitri Mendeleev; Lothar Meyer (independent parallel work)
summary: ndeleev arranged elements by atomic weight in 1869, leaving s for undiscovered elements whose properties he predicted h remarkable accuracy (later confirmed).
key_facts: All three predicted elements confirmed by 1886

name: Quantum Mechanics
period: 1900–1930s (Planck, Einstein, Bohr, Heisenberg, Schrödinger, Dirac)
key_figures: Max Planck; Niels Bohr; Werner Heisenberg; Erwin Schrödinger; Paul Dirac; Albert Einstein
summary: antum mechanics describes all atomic and subatomic phenomerpaanck's quantum hypothesis: 1900 the most precisely tested theory in physics, with dictionsverified to one part in ten billion.

name: Theory of Relativity
period: 1905 (Special Relativity); 1915 (General Relativity)
key_figures: Albert Einstein
summary: ecial relativity (1905) gave us E=mc². General relativity 15) described gravity as spacetime curvature, predicting vitational waves, black holes, and time dilation.
key_facts: GPS requires relativistic corrections to work
Gravitational waves detected by Li GO: 2015

name: Nuclear Energy and Nuclear Science
period: 1938 (nuclear fission discovered); 1942 (first controlled chain reaction); 1951 (first electricity from nuclear power)
key_figures: Otto Hahn, Lise Meitner, Fritz Strassmann (fission); Enrico Fermi (reactor); Manhattan Project team
summary: Nuclear fission was discovered in December 1938. Fermi achieved Fi the first controlled chain reaction in 1942. Nuclear power today provides ~10%of world electricity
key_facts: First controlled chain reaction in 1942. Nuclear power

name: Plastics and Synthetic Materials
period: 1907 (Bakelite, first fully synthetic plastic); 1930s–1950s (nylon, polyethylene, Teflon)
key_figures: Leo Baekeland (Bakelite); Wallace Carothers (nylon); DuPont chemists
summary: kelite (1907) was the first fully synthetic plastic. Nylon, yethylene, PVC, and Teflon followed in the 1930 s-40 s. Global duction now exceeds 40 o M tonnes per year.
key_facts: Nylon developed by Carothersat Du Pont: 1935

name: Fertilisers and Modern Agricultural Chemistry
period: 1909 (Haber-Bosch process for synthetic ammonia); widespread use from 1950s Green Revolution
key_figures: Fritz Haber; Carl Bosch (BASF); Norman Borlaug (Green Revolution)
summary: Haber-Bosch process (1909) synthesises ammonia from hospheric nitrogen, making synthetic fertiliser possible. f the nitrogen in human bodies now comes from this process.
key_facts: Enabled 3 x increase in food production
Green Revolution saved~l B peoplefrom famine
Nitrogen runoff causes major water pollution

name: Telegraph
period: 1837 (Cooke and Wheatstone, UK); 1844 (Morse's Baltimore–Washington line)
key_figures: Samuel Morse; Alfred Vail; William Cooke; Charles Wheatstone
summary: telegraph separated communication from physical transport the first time. The transatlantic cable (1866) connected ope and North America almost instantly.
key_facts: Created Reuters, AP, and financial wire services

name: Telephone
period: 1876 (Bell's patent); widespread adoption from 1890s–1920s
key_figures: Alexander Graham Bell; Elisha Gray (disputed co-inventor); Antonio Meucci
summary: I's patent (7 March 1876) became one of the most nmercially valuable in history. AT&T, founded from Bell's npany, dominated Us communications for a century.
key_facts: Legal disputes with Gray and Meucci
Became AT&T, dominated US fora century

name: Radio
period: 1895–1901 (Marconi's wireless telegraphy); broadcasting from 1920s
key_figures: Guglielmo Marconi; Nikola Tesla; Heinrich Hertz (discovery of radio waves, 1887)
summary: Hertz proved electromagnetic waves exist in 1887. Marconi's first transatlantic radio signal (1901) opened the era of wireless communication and eventually mass broadcasting.
key_facts: Radio was decisive in both World Wars
Underpins Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, GPS, mobile phones

name: Transistor
period: 1947 (invention at Bell Labs); widespread commercial use from mid-1950s
key_figures: William Shockley, John Bardeen, Walter Brattain (Bell Labs)
summary: entedat Bell Labson 23 December 1947, thetransistor laced vacuum tubes. Modem processor chips contain over 100 on transistors on a fingemail-sized die.

name: Integrated Circuit / Microchip
period: 1958–1959 (independent inventions by Kilby and Noyce)
key_figures: Jack Kilby (Texas Instruments); Robert Noyce (Fairchild Semiconductor)
summary: y (12 Sep 1958) and Noyce independently placed multiple nsistorson a single chip. This enabled the exponential t/size reduction described by Moore's Law.
key_facts: Intel founded by Noyce and Moore: 1968

name: Computer
period: 1936–1945 (theoretical foundations and first digital computers); commercial era from 1950s
key_figures: Alan Turing (theoretical foundation); Konrad Zuse (Z3, 1941); ENIAC team (1945); John von Neumann
summary: ng's 1936 theoretical foundation and the first programmable nputers (Z 3, ENi AC) established computing. Von Neumann's hitecturebecame thestandard designfor all computers.
key_facts: Universal machine: same hardware, any software

name: Internet
period: 1969 (ARPANET); 1970s–1980s (TCP/IP protocols); 1990s (commercial internet)
key_figures: ARPA/DARPA team; Vint Cerf and Bob Kahn (TCP/IP, 1974); Tim Berners-Lee (WWW, 1989–1991)
summary: PANET (1969) connected four US research universities. Cerf Kahn's TCP/IP protocol (1974) allowed different networks to nmunicate, creating the internet as we know it.

name: World Wide Web
period: 1989–1991 (proposal and first implementation); 1993 (Mosaic browser, public adoption)
key_figures: Tim Berners-Lee; Robert Cailliau; Marc Andreessen (Mosaic browser)
summary: istmas 1990. Made freely available without patents, it ame the largest expansion of communication since print.
key_facts: No patents: made available to all freely

name: Search Engines
period: 1990 (Archie, first internet search tool); 1998 (Google founded)
key_figures: Alan Emtage (Archie, 1990); Larry Page and Sergey Brin (Google, 1998)
summary: gle's Page Rank algorithm (1998) ranked pages by link quality her than keywords alone, dramatically improving relevance Itransforming how humanity accessesknowledge.
key_facts: Alta Vista, Lycos, Yahoo: mid-1990 s
Al chatbots now beginning to challenge search

name: Satellite Technology
period: 1957 (Sputnik 1, first artificial satellite); 1963 (first geostationary communication satellite)
key_figures: Soviet space programme (Sputnik); NASA; Arthur C. Clarke (geostationary orbit concept, 1945)
summary: Itnik 1 (4 Oct 1957) was the first artificial satellite. rkeproposedgeostationarycommunicationssatellites in 5. Over 9, 000 satellites are now in orbit.

name: GPS (Global Positioning System)
period: 1973 (concept); 1978 (first satellites launched); 1995 (full operational capability)
key_figures: US Department of Defense; Roger L. Easton, Ivan Getting, Bradford Parkinson
summary: US Do D's GPS reached full operational capability in 1995 ective Availability(intentional degradation)removed in 0, enabling civilian metre-level accuracy globally.
key_facts: Also: GLONASS, Galileo, Bei Dou systems
Economic value to US economy: $l T+

name: Semiconductor Technology
period: 1947 (transistor); 1958 (IC); 1960s–present (Moore's Law scaling)
key_figures: Bell Labs; Texas Instruments; Intel; TSMC; ASML
summary: m the first transistor to chips with 1 o 0 B+ transistors in 60 rs, semiconductor scaling (Moore's Law) has driven the most tained technological improvement in history.

name: Laser
period: 1960 (first working laser, Theodore Maiman); 1961 (first medical laser application)
key_figures: Theodore Maiman (first ruby laser, 1960); Charles Townes and Arthur Schawlow (theoretical basis, MASER)
summary: man built the first working laser in May 1960. Eary lasers re called 'a solution looking for a problem'*; by 2024 they lerpin medicine, communications, and manufacturing.
key_facts: Used in LASi K, fibre optics, manufacturing
Li DAR in self-driving vehicles uses lasers

name: Fibre-Optic Communication
period: 1966 (Kao and Hockham's theoretical paper); 1970 (Corning's low-loss glass fibre); commercial deployment from 1980s
key_figures: Charles Kao (Nobel Prize 2009); George Hockham; Robert Maurer, Donald Keck, Peter Schultz (Corning)
summary: Kao and Hockham’s 1966 paper proposed ultra-pure glass fibres for optical communication.Coming achieved the required purity in 1970. Kao received the Nobel Prize in 2009.

name: Spaceflight
period: 1957 (Sputnik); 1961 (first human in space, Gagarin); 1969 (Moon landing)
key_figures: Sergei Korolev (Soviet); Wernher von Braun (US); NASA; Yuri Gagarin; Neil Armstrong
summary: itnik (1957), Gagarin (1961), and Apollo 11 (1969) defined Space Age. Commercial spaceflight (Space X, 2010 s) is now haping access to orbit.
key_facts: Gagarin: first human in space: 12 April 1961

name: Smartphones
period: 1992 (IBM Simon, first smartphone concept); 2007 (iPhone, mass market transformation)
key_figures: IBM (Simon, 1994); Apple (iPhone, 2007, Steve Jobs); Google (Android, 2008)
summary: i Phone(2007)transformed the smartphone from a niche ice into a mass-market platform. Android (2008) brought nparable capability to all price points.

name: Social Media Platforms
period: 1997 (SixDegrees, first recognisable social network); 2004 (Facebook); 2006 (Twitter)
key_figures: Mark Zuckerberg (Facebook); Jack Dorsey (Twitter); Kevin Systrom (Instagram); multiple others
summary: ebook (2004) reached 1 billion usersby 2012. Social media sformed political communication, journalism, and activism le also enabling disinformation andpolarisation.

name: Artificial Intelligence
period: 1950 (Turing's test); 1956 (Dartmouth Conference, AI coined); current boom from 2012
key_figures: Alan Turing; John McCarthy; Marvin Minsky; Geoffrey Hinton; Yann LeCun; Yoshua Bengio
summary: Deep learning's breakthrough (AlexNet, 2012) triggered the modern AI era. AlphaFold (2020) solved the 50-year protein structure problem; LLMs now write, code, and reason.

name: Renewable Energy Technologies
period: 1954 (first practical silicon solar cell); 1970s–present (wind, solar, storage development)
key_figures: Bell Labs (silicon solar cell, 1954); Darrieus and other wind turbine pioneers; multiple researchers
summary: Solar PV cost has fallen 99% since 1976. Solar and wind provided ~13% of global electricity in 2023, up from near zero in 2000, and are projected to dominate by the 2030s.
key_facts: First practical silicon solar cell: Bell Labs, 1954
IEA projects majority of electricity by 2030 s

name: Battery Technology
period: 1800 (Volta's pile, first battery); 1991 (Sony's first commercial lithium-ion battery)
key_figures: Alessandro Volta (electrochemical battery, 1800); John Goodenough, M. Stanley Whittingham, Akira Yoshino (lithium-ion battery — Nobel Prize 2019)
summary: lithium-ion battery, commercialised by Sony in 1991, has en in cost by ~97% since then. It is the enabling hnology for electric vehicles and renewable energy storage.

name: Electric Vehicles
period: 1880s (early EVs); 1990s (GM EV1); 2008–present (Tesla and the modern EV era)
key_figures: Charles Jeantaud (France, 1881); GM EV1 (1996); Elon Musk / Martin Eberhard (Tesla, 2003)
summary: predate ICE cars, but Tesla's Roadster (2008) showed high- formance long-range EVswereviable. Global EV sales passed 10 M/year by 2022.
key_facts: Early EVs predated gasoline cars: 1880 s

name: Robotics
period: 1961 (Unimate, first industrial robot); 1970s (automated manufacturing); 21st century (AI-enabled robotics)
key_figures: George Devol (Unimate, 1961); Joseph Engelberger; Boston Dynamics; Rodney Brooks; Masahiro Mori
summary: firstindustrialrobot, Unimate, wasinstalled at GM in 61 for spot welding. Al-enabled robots now work in surgery stics, agriculture, and space exploration.
key_facts: Industnial robotsspread in auto industry: 1970 s-90 s

name: 3D Printing (Additive Manufacturing)
period: 1984 (Chuck Hull's stereolithography patent); commercial use from 1990s; mainstream from 2010s
key_figures: Chuck Hull (stereolithography, 1984); Carl Deckard (SLS); Scott Crump (FDM, Stratasys)
summary: I's 1984 stereolithography patent began 3 D printing. FDM d drove consumer printer costs from 14, 000 to 200.
key_facts: Used for titanium implants, jet engine parts
3 D bioprinting of tissue: emerging field

name: Blockchain
period: 2008 (Bitcoin whitepaper by Satoshi Nakamoto); 2015 (Ethereum smart contracts)
key_figures: Satoshi Nakamoto (pseudonym, identity unknown); Vitalik Buterin (Ethereum)
summary: kamoto's 2008 whitepaper introduced a decentralised, tamper- Bitcoin whitepaper: 0 ctober 2008 stantledger. Ethereum(2015)addedprogrammablesmart tracts, extending blockchain beyond currency.
key_facts: Applications: supply chain, identity, De Fi

name: Cloud Computing
period: 2006 (Amazon Web Services launch); 2008–2010 (Google Cloud, Microsoft Azure); mainstream from 2010s
key_figures: Amazon Web Services (Jeff Bezos, Andy Jassy); Salesforce (Marc Benioff, early SaaS); Google; Microsoft
summary: Amazon Web Services (2006) offered on-demand computing as a utility. AWS, Azure, and Google Cloud now generate over $250B revenue annually and host most internet services.
key_facts: Democratised access to AI and computing
